meta.title: The Absentee Owner — Playable Case Study
meta.hubTagline: The owner-first, fiduciary care service that keeps Mumbai, Alibaug and Goa second homes guest-ready — and monetises them only if you want. StayVista rents your villa; we protect it.

hero.sub: A playable walk-through of the Second-Home & NRI Property Management dossier. Same facts, same numbers — but you get to investigate, decide, and vote.

opening.lead: Every scaled operator a wealthy owner has heard of — StayVista, Lohono, SaffronStays — earns money only when your home is let. Their incentive is occupancy, not the care of your asset. Nobody sells the owner fiduciary care as the product. That single gap is the whole thesis.

stats.labelHtml: of Indian buyers now prefer real estate over stocks, gold & FDs (ANAROCK H1 2025) — demand is real
NRI real-estate investment in FY21; NRI share of purchases has climbed to ~18–20% and needs caring for
Owner-first fiduciary care operators for premium coastal second homes — the whitespace, quantified
Homes under management to break even EST. — an asset-light threshold, reachable in the Mumbai + Alibaug cluster

imgband.title: Three ways to leave your home behind
imgband.sub: Rental operators who earn only when it is let, on undisclosed 25–40% revenue-shares · informal caretakers, relatives and watchmen nobody is accountable to · inspection-only point solutions. The owner-first middle is empty.

whitespace.lead: Ten real players, plotted. Hover any dot. Notice where nobody is: premium-priced and owner-first care — a retainer to protect the asset, not a cut of the rent. That corner is what this business is built to own.

players.d: $130M group; ultra-luxury rental mgmt, opaque terms
villa rental mgmt; ₹87cr Series B / ₹40cr bootstrapped
10–12% / 7–9% of rent; metro long-let, not coastal
fixed ₹7.5k–13.5k/yr retainer, but 3 South cities, tenancy
inspection/diligence point solutions, not ongoing care

whitespace.sidebarHtml:
Reading the map.

The funded, premium names — StayVista, Lohono, SaffronStays — all cluster bottom-left: high-end, but occupancy-first. HomeShikari is the only true fixed care retainer — but three South-India cities, tenancy-focused, sub-premium.

The top-right — premium and owner-first — is uncontested. That is the investable idea.

hook.titleHtml: Who actually earns when your home sits safely empty?
hook.lead: Every one of these looks like it manages your home. Diligence asks one question of each: do they make money protecting an asset that is not let? Click each case file to see the verdict — and watch the whitespace open up.
hook.statusInitialTail: open them all to close the case.
hook.statusSolvedHtml: Case closed. Every scaled, premium operator fails the alignment test — they earn only when your home is let. HomeShikari proves owners will pay a clean fixed retainer, but in the wrong cities and the wrong segment. The owner-first, premium, coastal box is empty — the whitespace is real.

cases.name: StayVista
cases.clue: "~1,000 villas — surely they protect the asset."
cases.det: Earns a 30% commission (70:30 split) — money only when your home is rented. Occupancy-first, with documented double-booking and refund complaints, and loss-making. Aligned with bookings, not with your asset.

cases.name: Lohono / Isprava
cases.det: A build-plus-rental model on an undisclosed monthly revenue-share. Premium and polished — but opaque, premium-only, and monetisation-first. It cannot sell a protect-the-asset retainer without fighting its own model.

cases.name: Informal caretaker
cases.clue: "The watchman and a cousin already live there."
cases.det: NoBroker's own words: prone to "poor maintenance, inadequate tenant screening, and a lack of regular updates," with vacant homes exposed to unauthorised occupation and forged documentation. Free — and precisely the low-trust layer this business displaces.

cases.name: NoBroker / Anarock
cases.det: Charge 8–12% of rent — metro-flat, long-let optimised. Their whole economics ride on the property being tenanted; a staffed coastal villa on a protect-first retainer is a different customer and a different ops muscle entirely.

cases.name: HomeShikari
cases.det: The single operator publishing a fixed care retainer (₹7,499–13,499/yr) — proof that owners will pay a transparent, occupancy-independent fee. But it is three South cities and tenancy-focused, not premium coastal second homes. It validates the model without filling the box.

personas.titleHtml: Four owners who can't be there.
personas.lead: Every persona has a trigger, an objection, and one thing that converts them. Flip each card to see what wins the sale.

items.name: Kabir Mehta, 45
items.role: The Weekend Baron · Mumbai HNI, Alibaug villa
items.trig: Handover of a new Alibaug villa he'll use 30–50 nights a year; the guilt and hassle of coordinating a ₹5.5–12 lakh staffing bill blind and remotely.
items.obj: "Why pay a retainer when my caretaker and watchman already live on-site?" — plus the quiet fear he's being robbed of things he'll never notice.
items.conv: Time-stamped photographic proof in the owner app + bonded, insured staff + optional rental upside on idle weekends without ever dealing with guests.

items.name: Priya Nair, 38
items.role: The Remote Landlord · NRI (US/Gulf), rented Mumbai flat
items.trig: Delayed rent, a fear of unauthorised subletting, and TDS/society confusion from eight time zones away.
items.obj: Already burned by informal caretakers who send no updates — skeptical that anyone managing remotely is truly accountable.
items.conv: Transparent app reporting, rent collected into her NRO account, and 31.2% TDS / FEMA / Lower-TDS (Form 13) compliance handled end-to-end.

items.name: Col. Deshpande, 67
items.trig: Health and travel now limit visits; monsoon damage anxiety; zero interest in ever renting to strangers.
items.obj: Budget-aware and wary of slick operators — he just wants reliable pure upkeep and a familiar point of contact.
items.conv: A standalone care-only retainer with no rental strings, a fixed relationship manager, security and monsoon-proofing — exactly the product the rental-first market refuses to sell him.

items.name: Neel Shah, 40
items.trig: Bought purely for returns; wants maximum occupancy and clean, trustworthy payout statements.
items.obj: Will churn to whoever delivers RevPAR, and is suspicious of opaque revenue-shares after dealing with the rental operators.
items.conv: Demonstrable rental performance + a transparent 20–25% commission (below StayVista's 30%) + clean statements paid straight into his account.

tycoon.lead: Four real decisions from the dossier. Each has a right answer the diligence already argues for. Pick — then see what the evidence says.

decisions.q: 1 · How do you earn your money?
decisions.ctx: The scaled players all take a cut of the rent. Choose your revenue model.

opts.t: Rental commission only — earn when the home is let
opts.fb: That's the StayVista/Lohono model — and its conflict of interest. Your incentive becomes occupancy, not care, and you have nothing to sell the ~third of owners who never want tenants. You'd be copying the box everyone already fills.

opts.t: A recurring care retainer, with rental as an option
opts.fb: Correct. A retainer to protect the asset is the owner-first box competitors can't copy without cannibalising their own occupancy-based model — and it's the higher-margin, stickier product.

decisions.q: 2 · When do you add trust controls?
decisions.ctx: Bonded staff, insurance, IoT cameras and tamper-evident inventory all cost money up front.

opts.t: Add them later, once you have scale and cash
opts.fb: The single biggest risk is theft or staff misconduct at a client home — one incident destroys the trust the whole model is sold on. Deferring the controls is deferring the product itself.

opts.t: Bonded staff, insurance & IoT from day one
opts.fb: Correct. Trust is the product, so the controls are day-one, not a later add-on — and the app's photographic proof directly attacks the buyer's number-one fear.

decisions.q: 3 · Which market do you open first?
decisions.ctx: Goa has the highest rental yield; Mumbai + Alibaug is Aamara's home turf.

opts.t: Launch in Goa first — chase the highest yield
opts.fb: Goa has the highest ops complexity and thinnest labour market — entered only once the playbook is proven. Leading with it means learning the hardest lessons before you have any density.

opts.t: Mumbai + Alibaug — density and home turf first
opts.fb: Correct. Highest density, shortest logistics loop, and Aamara's own network — prove the fiduciary model and reach cluster density before exporting the playbook to Lonavala, then Goa.

decisions.q: 4 · How do you handle NRI rent money?
decisions.ctx: An NRI client's rent must move under FEMA, with 31.2% TDS under Section 195.

opts.t: Touch the owner's rent directly to move fast
opts.fb: Mishandled TDS or repatriation triggers penalties on the owner — the exact trust-killer for an NRI. Never touch owner money without 15CA/CB compliance; speed here is a liability.

opts.t: Route everything through a partner CA network, 15CA/CB
opts.fb: Correct. A CA partner network turns FEMA/TDS friction into the highest-value NRI add-on — collecting into NRO, filing Form 27Q, and securing a Lower-TDS certificate the owner would dread doing alone.

human.titleHtml: The most important ₹ in the deck.
human.lead: The moat isn't a feature — it's an incentive. A rental operator earns nothing from a home that sits safely empty; their living depends on renting it out. This business earns its living from exactly the opposite: keeping the asset protected whether it's ever let or not.

rows.label: Rental operator's take when your home sits safely empty
Owner-first operator's retainer to protect that same home

human.note: Roughly a third of the target market — retirees and private-use HNIs — will never rent, and they are the stickiest, highest-margin customers (~55–60% GM EST.). The retainer isn't just aligned pricing; it's the only ₹ that exists for the owner the rental-first market cannot serve at all.

quiz.lead: Five questions. Then you cast the vote you'd cast in the room.

opts: No owner-first, fiduciary, transparent full-care service exists for premium coastal second homes — the scaled players all earn only when your home is let
Second homes are a brand-new asset class
items.e: StayVista, Lohono and SaffronStays monetise via 25–40% revenue-shares and earn only on occupancy. HomeShikari is the only clean retainer — but three South cities, tenancy-focused. The owner-first premium box is empty.

items.q: Why do the rental operators fail the owner-first test?
opts: Their occupancy-based revenue structurally conflicts with a protect-the-asset retainer — they'd have to fight their own incentive
items.e: A true fiduciary retainer conflicts with pushing rentals; for StayVista or Lohono to offer it credibly they'd cannibalise their own occupancy-based model and brand. The positioning, not the feature, is the moat.

items.q: Roughly how many homes to break even?
items.e: At ~₹6–7 lakh blended gross revenue per home and ~40% margin against ~₹2.5–3 crore fixed overhead, break-even is ~100–130 homes EST. — reachable within the Mumbai + Alibaug cluster by Year 2–3.

items.q: What is the single biggest risk the dossier names?
opts: Theft or staff misconduct at a client home — a trust the entire model is sold on, destroyable by one incident
items.e: The whole model is sold on trust, so one theft or misconduct event can destroy it — which is why bonded staff, insurance and IoT must be day-one, not a later add-on.

items.q: Why should Aamara — a family office — run this rather than buy a proven business?
opts: Because the owner-first version doesn't exist yet — the absence IS the opportunity, and Aamara's Mumbai HNI/NRI network plus local cluster density is the edge
items.e: The trusted-advisor and NRI-network channels, plus density earned home by home, are relationship assets a funded competitor can't buy — but the lead compounds while incumbents decide, which is the argument to move now.

vote.prompt: You've seen the whitespace, the alignment test, the personas, the unit economics and the human number. What's your call?
vote.yesMsg: Backed. Your Phase-1 first move (per the Action Plan): run 25–30 discovery interviews with Mumbai HNI Alibaug-villa owners and NRI flat owners through the Aamara / family-office network, lock the fiduciary positioning and retainer price points, and map the CRZ / legal diligence checklist — all inside the first 30 days. Asset-light, recurring, cluster-by-cluster capital.
vote.noMsg: Passed — and defensibly so. The dossier agrees the variance is real: a self-constructed, bottom-up TAM with no comparable that has raised institutional capital on the owner-first model, low switching costs early, thin coastal labour markets, and a business whose entire promise can be destroyed by a single theft or misconduct event. A pass here is a legitimate IC position — the case study never pretends otherwise.

footer.note1: This playable walk-through is a companion to the full institutional dossier — it simplifies for engagement and never overrides the source document.
footer.note2: Every number here is drawn verbatim from the dossier, including its EST. flags and indicative / trade-press caveats.
